title: Document Control Essentials for GMP
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical guide to document control GMP: document hierarchy, lifecycle and change control, ALCOA+ data integrity and common pitfalls for UK and EU quality teams.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. A gloved hand in soft focus turns a page of a precisely organised controlled document binder on a brushed-steel laboratory bench, neat tabbed dividers and a faint embossed grid catching soft natural side light. Shallow depth of field blurs sterile glassware and a blurred quality-control workstation in the background, conveying order, traceability and trust. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, crisp editorial realism.

faqs.q: What does EU GMP Chapter 4 require for document control?
faqs.a: EU GMP Chapter 4 treats good documentation as a fundamental part of the quality management system, distinguishing between instructions (procedures, specifications, protocols) and records (the completed evidence of work performed). It expects documents to be approved, signed and dated by authorised persons, available at the point of use, and retained for defined periods. The aim is a system in which any activity can be reconstructed and defended from the records alone.

faqs.q: How do ALCOA+ principles apply to GMP documentation?
faqs.a: ALCOA+ means every record should be Attributable, Legible, Contemporaneous, Original and Accurate, plus Complete, Consistent, Enduring and Available. In practice this drives contemporaneous entries, single-line corrections that preserve the original, signed and dated changes, and a ban on back-dating or correction fluid. For electronic systems the same expectations are met through validated software, controlled user access and audit trails that are actively reviewed.

faqs.q: How often should SOPs be reviewed under GMP?
faqs.a: There is no single mandated frequency, but a periodic review cycle of roughly every two to three years is common, adjusted according to risk using ICH Q9 principles. Critical, high-risk procedures may warrant more frequent review, while stable low-risk forms can be reviewed less often. Any change in process, equipment, regulation or following a deviation should also trigger an out-of-cycle review through formal change control.
